---
title: "Breeze Buddy Voice Stack"
subtitle: "A technical brief on building production voice AI for India"
author: "Breeze Buddy Engineering"
date: today
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-location: left
    toc-title: "On this page"
    number-sections: true
    theme: cosmo
    code-fold: false
    code-tools: false
    smooth-scroll: true
    link-external-newwindow: true
    fig-cap-location: bottom
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
---

# Executive Summary {.unnumbered}

The voice-AI market is past the novelty phase. Raw model access is commoditising quickly — every major platform now ships real-time speech models, SIP calling, tool use, and structured output. The durable advantage is no longer "we have the model." It is the system wrapped around the model: orchestration, conversational quality tuned for the real world, runtime tuned for live sessions, and feedback loops that turn every call into signal.

Breeze Buddy is that system. This document describes the voice stack we have built inside Clairvoyance, explains the design choices that are load-bearing (and why), and is honest about the parts that are still unfinished.

Our thesis is narrow on purpose. We are not building the most sovereign Indian speech model, the broadest enterprise CX platform, or a general-purpose voice API. We are building **the best-engineered, fastest-moving voice operations layer for India-first enterprise workflows**. The product already serves enterprise merchants across commerce, travel and mobility, delivery, and customer-care operations, and financial services is the natural next expansion — regulated industries benefit most from the orchestration, audit trail, and runtime determinism we have built. That is where the gap between what general platforms offer and what the market actually needs is large, and where the engineering depth compounds.

The rest of this document walks through four capability layers we have invested in, each of which corresponds to a point of differentiation that survives the model commodification wave:

1. **Orchestration and flow control** — a template-driven, node-based state machine with LLM-decided but authoritative transitions and fire-and-forget side effects. Non-engineers ship new conversation flows in days; every call is auditable; state and behaviour are decoupled.
2. **India-specific conversational quality** — blended STT/TTS providers chosen per workflow, strict code-mix handling, payload-driven language selection, Indian number and regional phonetic handling, and noise filtering tuned for 8 kHz telephony.
3. **Runtime tuned for live sessions** — a one-pod-per-call deployment (Agent's Cloud) with merchant-specific warm pools, circuit-breakered orchestration, Nginx-anchored media streams, pluggable turn-detection strategies, interruption control, and a unified telephony + Daily.co transport layer so every call — phone or web — runs the same pipeline.
4. **Evaluation and iteration loops** — a reactive evaluation loop already running in production (LLM-as-judge scoring + Slack alerting with recording URLs), and a proactive loop (replay, A/B, pre-deployment regression) that we have the traces for but have not yet built.

At the end we describe where we think investment should go next: closing the proactive evaluation loop, specialised small models for bounded enterprise tasks, and a small, deliberate exploration of the speech-native (full-duplex) frontier.

---

# The Problem: Production Voice AI in India

## Where we have chosen not to spend our time

English, in our pipeline, is the part of the stack that is allowed to be reliable without being interesting. The production default already runs English-first enterprise workflows at scale today, and the quality of the model layer is no longer a bottleneck on what the product can do. That is a deliberate design choice: keep the easy part boring so the real engineering goes into the layers above the model — which is where the hard problems live, and where the product actually ships.

## What is not solved, at least not for India

The hard problems for production voice AI in India are not in the model layer. They are in the space between the model and a reliable, repeatable business outcome. Specifically:

- **Code-mixed speech.** A real customer says *"order ka status check kar sakte ho, please?"* — not pure Hindi, not pure English. Most STT providers either lock to a single language (garbling the mix) or auto-detect (drifting mid-utterance and missing entities).
- **Accent and dialect variance.** Bangalore English, Kolkata English, and Chennai English are phonetically different. Regional Hindi, Tamil, Telugu, Kannada, Marathi, Malayalam all have dialectal variation that models trained on web-scraped data under-represent.
- **Noisy telephony.** The real audio channel is 8 kHz µ-law over a mobile network, with background traffic, shop floors, kirana stores, and family conversations in the same room. Clean-audio benchmarks do not predict this.
- **Conversational determinism.** Real customers pause, backtrack, ask clarifying questions, interrupt, go off-topic, and produce partial answers. A giant-prompt approach is brittle: the LLM will sometimes skip a required step, sometimes ask the same question twice, and occasionally commit a business action with no audit trail. Enterprise workflows — especially regulated ones — need structured, auditable, resumable conversations.
- **Latency budget.** Voice conversation feels natural when the gap between "user stops" and "bot starts" is around 200 ms. A chat-class 1–2 second budget feels broken. That budget must be split across VAD, STT finalisation, LLM inference, TTS first byte, and the telephony leg. Every layer has to hit its number.
- **Volume with variance.** India has enormous sheet volume for voice interactions — commerce, delivery coordination, travel and mobility, collections, customer care, and increasingly regulated financial services — but every merchant's workflow is slightly different. "One prompt fits all" never survives production.

These are systems problems before they are model problems. Frontier models will keep improving, but they will not, on their own, solve multi-turn flow control, merchant-specific workflow templating, telephony resilience, interruption strategy, or evaluation infrastructure. Those layers are ours to build — and they are where the long-term advantage sits.

## The four layers where depth compounds

The rest of this document is organised around four capability layers. These are the layers where a general-purpose voice API stays shallow and where an India-first, enterprise-focused engineering team can build something durably better.

| Layer | What it means in practice |
|---|---|
| Orchestration and flow control | A template-driven state machine, LLM-decided transitions, async side effects, auditable outcomes |
| India-specific conversational quality | Blended STT/TTS per workflow, code-mix handling, payload-driven language selection, regional phonetics, noise filtering |
| Runtime tuned for live sessions | One-pod-per-call deployment (Agent's Cloud) with merchant-specific warm pools, circuit-breakered orchestration, Nginx-anchored media streams, pluggable turn-detection, interruption control, unified telephony + Daily.co transport |
| Evaluation and iteration loops | Full-resolution traces, LLM-as-judge scoring with Slack alerting, node traversal and outcome analytics — with a proactive replay/A-B loop still to build |

---

# Architecture at a Glance

## The layered view

A single call traverses six layers. Each layer is pluggable; templates select which provider or strategy runs at each level without any code changes.

```
+--------------------------------------------------------------+
|  Control plane (Clairvoyance Master, FastAPI)                |
|  /push/lead/v2 | /template | /webhook | make_call            |
+--------------------------------------------------------------+
              |
              v
+--------------------------------------------------------------+
|  Template layer  (Postgres-backed JSON)                      |
|  nodes | functions | hooks | global functions | configs      |
+--------------------------------------------------------------+
              |
              v
+--------------------------------------------------------------+
|  Voice-Orchestrator / Smart Router                           |
|  merchant-specific warm pools | pod allocation | circuit bkr |
+--------------------------------------------------------------+
              |
              v
+--------------------------------------------------------------+
|  Nginx router  (anchors media stream to the assigned pod)    |
+--------------------------------------------------------------+
              |
              v
+--------------------------------------------------------------+
|  Call pod  (one Kubernetes pod per live call)                |
|  Pipecat Flows | pipeline | transition handler | context     |
|                                                              |
|  transport_in -> STT -> turn detection -> LLM                |
|                                       -> TTS -> transport    |
+--------------------------------------------------------------+
              |
              v
+--------------------------------------------------------------+
|  Transport layer                                             |
|  Twilio | Plivo | Exotel  (telephony)  |  Daily.co  (web)    |
+--------------------------------------------------------------+
              |
              v
+--------------------------------------------------------------+
|  Network / customer device                                   |
+--------------------------------------------------------------+
```

Every live call is assigned its own Kubernetes pod. Templates are JSON rows in Postgres; operators edit them without touching code. Each provider at every layer (STT, TTS, LLM, transport) is a swap-in configuration — a template can choose Soniox + ElevenLabs + Twilio for one workflow and Sarvam + Cartesia + Exotel for another. The reasoning for the one-pod-per-call model is covered in detail in §[Runtime Tuned for Live Sessions]; the short version is that it is the only architecture we have found that gives deterministic latency under real production load.

## The pipeline frame flow

Inside the pipeline, audio and text flow through an ordered set of Pipecat processors. The order matters: this is where the real-time behaviour of the conversation is decided.

```
raw audio bytes
     |
     v
[ transport.input ]          <- mu-law 8kHz for telephony, PCM 16kHz for Daily
     |
     v
[ STT service ]              <- Soniox | Deepgram | Sarvam | Google | OpenAI
     |  (interim + final transcripts)
     v
[ transcription gate ]       <- optional mute/filter while bot speaks
     |
     v
[ user aggregator + VAD ]    <- turn detection: stt_native | smart_turn | timeout
     |                          interruption: enabled | disabled_discard | min_words
     v
[ LLM service ]              <- Azure OpenAI | Vertex Gemini | Claude on Vertex
     |  (text + function calls)
     v
[ TTS service ]              <- ElevenLabs | Sarvam | Cartesia | Google
     |
     v
[ assistant aggregator ]     <- context update, interruption handling
     |
     v
[ transport.output ]         <- mu-law 8kHz back to the telephony provider
```

When the LLM emits a function call, control leaves the pipeline and enters the template transition handler (see §[Orchestration and Flow Control]). When a transition happens, the flow manager swaps the node config underneath the LLM context and the conversation continues. When the user idles past a configured threshold, the idle processor fires a callback that can retry a prompt, escalate, or end the call.

::: {.callout-note}
**Why this structure is load-bearing.** Each processor has a single responsibility, and frames flow in one direction. Adding a new capability (a new noise filter, a new turn strategy, a new transcription gate) means adding a processor, not rewriting the pipeline. Every voice AI product eventually grows this shape. We started here on purpose.
:::

---

# Orchestration and Flow Control

This is the layer that most clearly separates a production voice product from a demo. A state machine with clear transitions and side effects is what lets non-engineers ship new flows safely, lets us debug failures by reading logs, and lets us evolve behaviour without rewriting the pipeline every time.

## Why a node-based flow, not a giant prompt

The three alternatives we rejected:

- **Giant prompt.** Put all the instructions in the system prompt and let the LLM drive the entire conversation. This is fast to prototype and impossible to operate. The LLM will sometimes skip mandatory steps, sometimes repeat itself, sometimes commit a business action without logging it. Worst of all, there is no structured outcome — you end up parsing free text to figure out what happened.
- **Procedural if/else.** Hand-code the entire flow in Python. Slow to ship, rigid, and every merchant-specific variation becomes a pull request.
- **General-purpose workflow engine.** Too heavy. The flow models in those systems are not built around LLM function calls and turn-by-turn conversation state.

We use **Pipecat Flows** as the underlying state machine and layer a JSON template on top. The template is the source of truth; the pipeline builds from it at call time. Transitions are LLM-decided (via function calls) but authoritative once taken, and side effects are async and non-blocking. This combination gives us both the flexibility of LLMs and the determinism of a state machine.

## What a template looks like

A template is a JSON document in Postgres with three layers: configuration (STT/TTS/VAD/interruption choices), nodes (the conversational state machine), and global functions (HTTP calls or built-in actions available from every node).

```json
{
  "id": "tpl-order-confirmation",
  "configurations": {
    "stt_configuration": {
      "provider": "soniox",
      "language": ["en", "hi"],
      "turn_detection": "stt_native",
      "soniox": { "context": "...domain JSON with product and merchant terms..." }
    },
    "tts_voice_name": "rhea",
    "vad_config": { "confidence": 0.5, "stop_secs": 0.3 },
    "interruption": { "mode": "enabled", "min_words": 2 }
  },
  "flow": {
    "initial_node": "greet",
    "nodes": [
      {
        "node_name": "greet",
        "task_messages": [
          { "role": "system",
            "content": "You are confirming {customer_name}'s order {order_id}." }
        ],
        "functions": [
          {
            "name": "confirm_order",
            "description": "Customer has confirmed they want to proceed.",
            "properties": { "confirmed": { "type": "boolean" } },
            "required": ["confirmed"],
            "transition_to": "confirmed",
            "hooks": [
              { "name": "update_outcome_in_database",
                "expected_fields": {
                  "outcome": { "source": "static", "value": "CONFIRMED" }
                } }
            ]
          }
        ]
      },
      { "node_name": "confirmed", "task_messages": [/* ... */], "functions": [] }
    ],
    "global_functions": [
      {
        "type": "http",
        "name": "check_inventory",
        "description": "Check if an item is in stock.",
        "http_request": {
          "method": "GET",
          "url": "https://inventory.example.com/check?item={item_id}",
          "timeout": 5,
          "max_retries": 2
        }
      }
    ]
  }
}
```

A few things to notice:

- Variables like `{customer_name}` and `{order_id}` are rendered at template **load** time from the lead payload, credentials, and template secrets — not at LLM time. This is deterministic, cheap, and auditable.
- `functions` attached to a node are the transitions the LLM can choose. Each function optionally specifies `transition_to` (where to go next) and `hooks` (async side effects).
- `global_functions` are callable from any node. HTTP global functions block the conversation until the external call returns; built-in ones route to internal handlers (warm transfer, end conversation, get current time, and so on).

## The transition handler, and why it matters

When the LLM calls a function, control lands in our **transition handler**. The design choice here is load-bearing.

1. **Hooks are scheduled as a fire-and-forget async task.** They do not block the transition. A slow database update or a flaky webhook never delays the next node.
2. **The transition itself is authoritative and immediate.** If the function specifies `transition_to`, we record the node exit, swap the VAD/interruption config to any node-level overrides, construct the next node's config, record entry, and hand control back to the flow manager. No conditional logic based on hook success.
3. **If the function does not specify a `transition_to`, we stay on the node.** This is how global functions work: they return data (from an HTTP call or a built-in action) to the LLM, which uses the result to decide the next turn.

The effect is a conversation that feels crisp — the bot moves on the instant a decision is made — while the audit trail (outcomes, webhooks, database updates) catches up in the background.

## Hooks versus functions versus global functions

These three concepts are easy to confuse. The table clarifies their roles:

| Concept | Who invokes it | Blocking? | What it returns | Scope |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Node function | LLM (function call) | Yes, briefly | Determines the next node | Current node |
| Hook | Attached to a node function | No (fire-and-forget) | Nothing | Current node's function |
| Global function | LLM (function call) | Yes (waits for result) | Data to the LLM, no node change | Every node in the flow |

This separation is the reason we can ship complex integrations (CRM updates, external validations, webhooks) without making the conversation feel sluggish.

## Handler architecture

Handlers live in two directories. **Internal handlers** (`handlers/internal/`) cover warm transfer, end-of-conversation, STT mute/unmute, audio playback, and the built-in function dispatcher. **Transport handlers** (`handlers/transport/`) cover HTTP global functions — the path for integrations with a merchant's own systems.

Warm transfer is a good example of how handlers compose. When the LLM calls `connect_to_live_agent`:

1. The built-in dispatcher routes to the warm-transfer handler.
2. The handler fetches the outbound number and the transfer target from the template.
3. It sets a Redis flag for the telephony provider (keyed by `call_sid`) with a short TTL.
4. It calls the provider-specific conference service (Twilio, Plivo, or Exotel each has its own bridging mechanism).
5. On success, it invokes the end-of-conversation path to release the AI side cleanly.
6. Meanwhile, the end-of-conversation hook collects the transcription from the LLM message history and stores it on the lead.

Every step is the same regardless of which telephony provider you use. The provider abstraction is in the service layer.

## Playground overrides

Operators and product teams can override STT, TTS, VAD, and interruption configuration at runtime by inserting a lead with `is_playground=true` and a `configurations_override` payload. The override is applied at greeting synthesis and at pipeline build time. This is how we let merchants iterate on voice configuration without redeploying templates. It is a small feature with outsized effect on how quickly we can debug a conversation issue.

## The call lifecycle state machine

A lead moves through a small, deterministic state machine. This is the *outer* orchestration — how a lead becomes a call, becomes a conversation, becomes an outcome — wrapping the *inner* template flow (nodes, transitions, hooks) we covered above.

```
        +---------+
        | BACKLOG |<----------------------------+
        +----+----+                             |
             |  cron pick + Redis lock           |
             v                                   |
     +---------------+                           |
     |  pre-checks   |  fail-open per check      |
     +-------+-------+                           |
             |  pass                             |
             v                                   |
     +---------------+                           |
     | acquire number|                           |
     +-------+-------+                           |
             |                                   |
             v                                   |
     +-------+-------+   init-failure  +---------+
     | provider call |-----------------> retry w/
     +-------+-------+                 | fallback
             | connected                 +----+---+
             v                                |
     +---------------+                        |
     |  PROCESSING   |<-----------------------+
     +-------+-------+
             |
             v
     +---------------+        BUSY / NO_ANSWER
     | conversation  |-----------------+
     +-------+-------+                 |
             |                         v
             v                  +----+----+
     +---------------+          | BACKLOG |
     |   FINISHED    |          | (retry) |
     +---------------+          +---------+
```

A few reliability details worth calling out:

- **Atomic Redis-backed lock on lead pick-up**, so the same lead is never picked by two pods simultaneously. A stuck-call watchdog cleans up leads that have been in `PROCESSING` too long.
- **Per-check fail-open configuration.** Each external pre-check can be configured to `proceed` on external-API failure (fail-open) or `skip` (fail-closed). Fail-open is typical for low-risk lookups so the business does not stall when a third party goes down; regulated workflows default to fail-closed so compliance is never bypassed.
- **Retry policy for BUSY / NO_ANSWER outcomes.** The lead is rescheduled with an exponential backoff and a bounded attempt count.
- **Recording download and storage** is provider-specific and handled after the call ends, so the pipeline itself is never blocked on S3 or provider-API latency.
- **End-of-conversation callbacks** run the transcription-export path, the reporting webhook with retry, and any template-specific post-call hooks.

## Configuration layering

The configuration model has three layers, each of which can override the one below:

1. **Static config** from environment variables, loaded once at process start. This is the baseline — infrastructure endpoints, default providers, pool sizes, feature flags.
2. **Dynamic config** from Redis (populated by DevCycle and similar feature-flag systems), accessed via async functions that fall back to defaults. This is what lets us tune provider choices, thresholds, and per-tenant behaviour without deploying.
3. **Template-level config** embedded in each template, which is what operators and merchants edit. Template config overrides both of the above for any call using that template.

On top of these three layers, the playground override path (above) lets us experiment on a single lead without touching any of them.

This layering is the reason we can run many merchants on the same codebase with genuinely different voice behaviours — and is the reason new provider rollouts (a new Sarvam model, a new ElevenLabs voice) happen via a Redis flag change, not a deploy.

## Why this layer is a moat

Because it turns conversation design from an engineering task into an operational task. A new merchant workflow that would take a week of prompt engineering and pull requests in a prompt-only system takes a day (sometimes an afternoon) with templates. The cost of shipping a variant drops by an order of magnitude — and that is where the sheet volume of the Indian enterprise voice market, across commerce, travel, collections, customer care, and regulated financial services, becomes unlockable instead of daunting.

---

# India-Specific Conversational Quality

This section answers the question we hear most often from people outside the voice space: *"If English STT and TTS are basically solved, what is left for you to do?"* The honest answer is: most of it.

## English is the foundation, not the ceiling

Our default production pipeline is English-first: Soniox for STT, ElevenLabs for TTS, with Indian-English voice tuning. This is a deliberate choice. English-first is reliable today, it covers a large share of early-majority enterprise conversations across our customer base, and it gives us a stable baseline against which to measure the multilingual extensions. We do not treat English as a limit; we treat it as the part of the stack that is allowed to be boring so the rest of it can be interesting.

## Code-mixing is the real India problem

A representative consumer sentence from a production call looks like this:

> *"Hi, mera order kab ayega? Please delivery boy ko address confirm karwa dena, okay?"*

This is not Hindi. It is not English. It is a single utterance with five language switches and a regional-English interjection. Most STT providers handle this poorly in two distinctive ways:

- **Single-language mode** garbles the non-primary language. A provider locked to English will transcribe *"kab ayega"* as noise or as nonsense English words.
- **Auto-detect mode** drifts. The provider commits to a language at the start of the utterance and the rest of the sentence is transcribed in the wrong language.

Our approach is blended and explicit:

- **Soniox with strict language hints.** The default for English-first and English+Hindi workflows. The template passes `language_hints: ["en", "hi"]` with `language_hints_strict=true`, which forces the model to only emit those two languages. We pair this with a structured **context JSON** carrying merchant-specific terms (product names, status terminology, payment methods) so the model has domain grounding instead of general-web priors.
- **Sarvam saarika for code-mixed-first workflows.** When a workflow is primarily regional and lightly code-mixed with English, Sarvam's saarika model is purpose-built for this. We use it selectively where it is stronger than the alternatives.
- **Deepgram Nova-3 with `numerals=true`.** For workflows where spoken numbers dominate (amounts, order IDs, OTP readback), Deepgram's numeral handling — including Indian-style "lakh" and "crore" — is more reliable than any alternative at the time of writing.
- **Google and OpenAI.** Available as fallbacks and for specific language/workflow combinations.

The point is that **no single provider is "good enough" for every workflow in India**. Treating STT as a blended strategy — different providers for different templates, chosen at configuration time — is more expensive to operate than a single-vendor default, and produces meaningfully better outcomes in production.

::: {.callout-warning}
**The honest take on Sarvam.** Sarvam is improving quickly and has genuine strength in code-mixed Indian speech and regional TTS. For our enterprise workflows today it is a selective tool, not a default. The same will be true for any single-vendor bet for the foreseeable future. The win is being able to choose per workflow, measure per workflow, and switch per workflow — not betting the product on a single provider's roadmap.
:::

## TTS: three providers, three different wins

| Provider | When we reach for it |
|---|---|
| **ElevenLabs** | Default. Fast synthesis, consistent voice personality across calls, Indian residency endpoint available, EN-IN voice tuning. Good for most English-first and English+Hindi enterprise workflows. |
| **Sarvam** | Regional-language workflows where natural pronunciation matters most. Automatic script detection (Devanagari → Hindi, Telugu → Telugu, Tamil → Tamil, etc.) with region-specific phonetics. |
| **Cartesia** | When the workflow benefits from emotion control (neutral / warm / excited) or a speed multiplier (for complex instructions read slowly). Also useful for merchant-custom voices. |

TTS, like STT, is a per-template choice, and for workflows where language selection depends on the lead payload we support **LLM-driven voice selection** at call time.

## Payload-driven language selection

Many leads carry enough structure for us to infer language and regional tone before the call even starts. A customer's city, name, and order metadata are reliable signals. The lead insertion path takes the template configuration and the payload, and resolves:

- The primary language for the call (e.g. Kannada for a Bangalore delivery, Hindi for a Patna delivery).
- The TTS voice to use for that call.
- Regional phrasing guidance injected into the system prompt.

For Hindi, Tamil, Telugu, Kannada, Marathi, Bengali, Gujarati, Punjabi, Malayalam and Odia workflows, the system prompt is augmented with guidance to use regional expressions and formal second-person forms (*aap*, *meeru*, *neengal*) rather than direct translation of English conversational conventions. This small step — a few hundred tokens of prompt — is the difference between a bot that sounds translated and a bot that sounds local.

## Indian number handling

Amounts and quantities are a recurring failure point for generic voice pipelines. *"Twenty-three lakh"* is not parsed correctly by models trained on Western number conventions. *"Paanch sau rupaya"* mixes script and language. *"Ek thousand do sau"* is a 1200 expressed across two languages.

We handle this in two layers. First, provider-level: Deepgram's `numerals=true` is configured for number-heavy workflows, and Soniox's context JSON carries Indian-style numeric terminology. Second, the transformation-function registry (used at template render time and in post-processing) normalises Indian number formats into machine-friendly values before they are passed to downstream systems.

## Noisy environments

Production audio is 8 kHz µ-law over mobile networks. The audio has telephony band-limiting, network jitter, and real-world ambient noise (traffic, family conversations, shop floors). We handle this with:

- **Optional AI Coustics noise filtering** (AIC), applied pre-STT, toggled per template. Adds a small amount of latency in exchange for meaningfully better STT accuracy in noisy conditions.
- **Silero VAD parameters tuned for 8 kHz telephony**, with configurable confidence, start/stop seconds, and minimum volume. A Daily.co web call runs with different parameters than a Twilio call because the audio characteristics are different.
- **Provider-level endpoint detection** tuned through configuration (Soniox `max_endpoint_delay_ms`, Deepgram endpointing milliseconds, Sarvam high VAD sensitivity for noisy lines).

## Turn-taking: knowing when the user is done

Turn detection — the decision about when a user has finished speaking and it is the bot's turn — is the single most influential knob for how natural a voice conversation feels. Get it wrong in one direction and the bot interrupts the user mid-sentence; get it wrong in the other direction and the user is left wondering if the bot even heard them. For Indian conversations the problem is harder than for clean English: speakers pause more naturally mid-sentence, switch languages mid-sentence, and frequently append confirmations or asides that a naive endpoint detector treats as new utterances.

Turn detection is template-driven, with three modes a template can choose between per workflow:

| Mode | How it decides the user is done | Strengths | Weaknesses |
|---|---|---|---|
| `stt_native` (default) | STT provider emits a semantic end-of-turn signal | Fastest; provider-optimised; works well with Soniox | Depends on provider endpoint quality |
| `smart_turn` | Small on-device ONNX model (`SmartTurnAnalyzerV3`) analyses prosody and pauses | Handles natural hesitation well; provider-agnostic | Higher latency; some CPU cost |
| `timeout` | Timer-based: wait N seconds after the last finalised transcript | Simple; predictable; any provider | Artificial delay on every turn |

Internally, turn detection is not a single processor. It is a set of **start strategies** (what counts as "the user has started speaking?") and **stop strategies** (what counts as "the user is done?") attached to the user aggregator. Start strategies can be combined:

- **VAD-based start.** Silero VAD detects speech energy. Fast, but false-fires on background noise.
- **Transcription-based start.** Interim STT transcripts trigger the start. Catches soft speech that VAD would miss, at the cost of higher latency.
- **Minimum-words start.** The user must speak at least N words before a "start" fires. Combined with the other two, this is how we suppress single-word backchannels — *"achha"*, *"hmm"*, *"theek hai"*, *"haan"* — that are extremely common in Indian conversational patterns and that a naive detector treats as interruptions.

Stop strategies are mutually exclusive per mode:

- **Accumulating-speech timeout.** Wait N seconds after the last finalised transcript. Used for `stt_native` (0.0 s — immediate) and `timeout` (configurable).
- **Turn-analyser stop.** Feeds accumulated audio into the SmartTurn ONNX model, which predicts "user is done" from prosody. Used for `smart_turn`. When no external VAD is provided, SmartTurn auto-creates a Silero VAD with `stop_secs=0.2` as its trigger.

Being able to choose a mode — and combine start/stop strategies — per workflow is what lets us say "for an address-collection flow where users speak slowly with pauses, use `smart_turn` with a minimum-words start" and "for a quick confirmation flow, use `stt_native` for minimum latency". A single-strategy product has to pick a compromise; a template-driven product does not.

## Interruption handling: barge-in without chaos

Interruption handling is how the bot distinguishes "the user is correcting me" from "the user just said *'achha'* while I was speaking". Three modes, template-level, overridable per node:

- **`enabled`** — the bot stops mid-sentence when the user speaks and the LLM gets the interruption. This is the default human-feeling mode.
- **`disabled_discard`** — user audio frames are dropped while the bot is speaking. Useful when the bot is reading critical information (terms, amounts, OTPs, account details) and a mid-sentence interruption would be a business hazard — particularly relevant for regulated workflows.
- **`enabled` + `min_words`** — the user must speak at least N words before the interruption fires. This is the setting that stops backchannel interjections from hijacking the conversation mid-TTS, which is extremely common in Indian calls.

## Where frontier models cannot catch up fast

Frontier models are trained predominantly on public web-scale data, which under-represents India-specific speech — and especially under-represents code-mixed, accented, noisy-telephony Indian speech. Every call we run produces a trace that nobody else has. That data does not become advantage on its own; it becomes advantage when the evaluation loop (next-but-one section) is closed.

---

# Runtime: Agent's Cloud

Voice is a real-time system, not a request/response system. The conversational latency budget is roughly 200 ms between *"user stops speaking"* and *"bot starts speaking"* to feel natural. That budget has to cover VAD detection, STT finalisation, LLM inference, TTS first byte, and the telephony round trip. Every layer must hit its number or the conversation feels broken.

But the per-turn latency budget is only half the story. The harder problem is making latency **predictable under real production load** — not on a lab benchmark, not with one call in the system, but across thousands of concurrent sessions with unpredictable CPU spikes, garbage collection pauses, noisy neighbours, and provider hiccups. The architectural choice that decides whether a voice product works in production is the answer to a simple question: *how do you isolate one call from another?*

## Why isolation is a correctness requirement, not an optimisation

Real-time audio has strict latency determinism requirements, and shared compute environments create fundamental problems: unpredictable latency spikes, CPU contention, memory pauses, fault propagation, and debugging complexity. We evaluated three concurrency models before settling on the one we run in production.

### Scenario 1 — Many calls per pod, asyncio (the naive approach)

Handle multiple concurrent WebSocket streams in a single Python process using `asyncio`. This is the obvious starting point, and it is wrong for real-time voice.

Python's Global Interpreter Lock means only one thread executes bytecode at a time. `asyncio` is excellent at I/O waiting, but any CPU-intensive task — VAD inference, audio resampling, µ-law encoding, tokenisation — blocks the entire event loop. If ten calls are sharing one process and one of them hits a heavy computation window, the event loop freezes for all ten users. The audible result is robotic voice, stuttering, jitter, and a broken human-like flow.

Adding more CPU cores to the pod does not fix this: the event loop is single-threaded. You can add cores, but you cannot add teachers.

::: {.callout-note}
**Analogy.** One teacher handling ten students. If one student asks a hard question, the teacher must focus on that student while the other nine wait silently. Adding more chairs does not give you more teachers.
:::

### Scenario 2 — Many calls per pod, subprocess per call

Spawn a separate OS subprocess for each call inside the same pod. Each subprocess has its own interpreter and its own GIL, so multiple cores can actually be used. This is better than asyncio, and it is still not enough.

The reason is **container boundary enforcement**. Kubernetes enforces CPU limits at the container level, not the process level. If one subprocess bursts CPU usage inside a 100 ms window, it can consume the entire pod's CPU quota for that window, and the Linux Completely Fair Scheduler (CFS) then throttles *the entire container*. Every other subprocess in that pod pauses temporarily — even ones that were idle — producing micro audio freezes and non-deterministic latency.

::: {.callout-note}
**Analogy.** The classroom now has helper teachers (subprocesses), one per student. But the classroom has only one electricity breaker (the pod's CPU quota). When one helper turns on a heavy heater, the breaker trips for the whole room, and every student's lights flicker — even the ones who were doing nothing.
:::

### Scenario 3 — One call per pod (what we run)

Each call is assigned its own Kubernetes pod with dedicated CPU and memory (for example 1 vCPU / 1 GiB per pod, sized per merchant tier). This is the architecture we run in production. It gives us four properties that the other scenarios do not:

- **Deterministic latency boundaries.** A CPU spike in one call cannot throttle another call.
- **Garbage-collection isolation.** Python stop-the-world GC pauses are limited to that one session.
- **Fault isolation.** If an audio driver crashes, a tool call segfaults, or an OOM happens, only that one call dies — every other call continues uninterrupted.
- **Operational simplicity.** One set of logs, one resource accounting, one lifecycle per call. Debugging is dramatically simpler.

### Trade-off summary

| Property | Shared pod (asyncio) | Shared pod (subprocess) | **One pod per call** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Latency consistency | Very low | Medium | **High** |
| Audio jitter | High | Medium | **Minimal** |
| Fault isolation | None | None | **Perfect** |
| Debuggability | Complex | Complex | **Simple** |
| Production safety | Low | Medium | **High** |
| Real-time suitability | Poor | Risky | **Industry standard** |

The one-pod-per-call pattern is what Pipecat Cloud recommends ("one bot per session"), what LiveKit Agents does ("isolate jobs per container"), and what Google Agones is built for ("session-based isolation at massive scale"). Real-time voice systems share most of their constraints with multiplayer game servers — stateful sessions, low-latency tolerance, strict isolation — and the same architectural pattern wins in both domains for the same reasons.

## Agent's Cloud: our one-pod-per-call runtime

The production implementation of this pattern is what we call **Agent's Cloud**. A single call flows through it like this:

1. **Clairvoyance Master (the control plane)** receives the lead and issues a `make_call` to the telephony provider.
2. **Voice-Orchestrator (Smart Router)** receives the corresponding session-setup request, identifies the merchant, and selects an available pod from that merchant's warm pool. It returns a unique WebSocket URL.
3. **Telephony provider** (Twilio, Plivo, Exotel) initiates the media stream to that WebSocket URL. For web calls, the Daily.co transport does the same thing with a Daily room URL.
4. **Nginx router** receives the media stream and anchors it directly to the specific pod assigned to that session.
5. **The call pod** runs the full Pipecat pipeline end to end for the duration of the call: transport → STT → turn detection → LLM → TTS → transport. The pod is dedicated to exactly one call.
6. **On call end**, the pod is recycled back into the warm pool or terminated, depending on its current state and merchant tier.

The Smart Router client is wrapped in a **circuit breaker** so that a brief router outage does not take every new call with it: when the router fails consecutively beyond a threshold, the breaker opens, new calls fall back to a direct (non-isolated) path, and the breaker gradually transitions back through half-open to closed as health recovers. Graceful degradation over hard failure.

## Merchant-specific warm pools

A cold Kubernetes pod costs 30–60 seconds to start: scheduling, image pull, model loading, interpreter warm-up, dependency wiring. Thirty seconds of silence at the start of a voice call is a conversation that will never happen. So the orchestrator maintains pre-initialised, warm pods waiting for assignment — the voice equivalent of a server keeping a connection pool hot.

The pools are **merchant-specific** on purpose:

- Different merchants run different templates with different STT/TTS/LLM combinations. Pre-warming a generic pod and reconfiguring it per call defeats the point.
- Merchant tier drives pod size. A simple COD confirmation bot runs on a lightweight pod; a heavy multi-tool workflow runs on a larger pod. Same architecture, different capacity per tier.
- Zombie-pod cleanup runs automatically: pods that are stuck in an unhealthy state or have exceeded their lifecycle budget are reclaimed so the pool always represents real, usable capacity.

The operational target is on the order of 10,000+ calls per hour with roughly 40 concurrent calls per minute, and the pool-sizing math is a straightforward function of concurrency, average call duration, and merchant mix. The total CPU and memory we use across the cluster is comparable to a vertically-scaled design — the difference is that the resources are distributed horizontally so isolation is free.

## Telephony and Daily.co: one transport layer, four providers

Buddy is a telephony-first product. The call channels we care about are mobile phone calls and, for specific web/mobile workflows, browser audio via Daily.co. The transport layer presents a unified interface to the rest of the stack so that a template does not know or care which channel it is running on.

| Provider | Channel | Audio format | Where it fits |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Twilio** | Telephony (outbound & inbound) | µ-law 8 kHz, Twilio Media Streams | Primary and most widely used; strong international reach |
| **Plivo** | Telephony | µ-law 8 kHz, custom WebSocket | Alternative provider with its own routing and pricing strengths |
| **Exotel** | Telephony | µ-law 8 kHz, custom WebSocket | Strong India presence, per-number channel management |
| **Daily.co** | Web / mobile browser | PCM 16 kHz, Daily transport | Web and in-app voice, also used by the product team for playground testing |

Every one of these providers has its own WebSocket protocol, its own webhook format, its own recording API, and its own failure modes. Abstracting them behind a single transport interface is grunt work that pays for itself every time a merchant asks to switch provider (for cost or compliance reasons) and we do it with a configuration change instead of a code change.

What we get from this abstraction in production:

- **Per-call failover across telephony providers.** A call initiated on the primary provider that fails to connect falls back to a secondary provider automatically. The merchant sees "the call went through"; they do not see the provider choice.
- **Unified recording, transcription and outcome pipelines.** Recordings are downloaded post-call via the provider-specific API, stored centrally, and surfaced through the same alerting and analytics paths regardless of which provider ran the call.
- **Number-level rate limiting** with a Redis sliding-window counter. Per-destination rate limits are tracked, alerted on Slack when exceeded, and — on a per-number configurable toggle — can block further calls outright to protect deliverability reputation.
- **Warm transfer across all three telephony providers.** When the LLM calls the `connect_to_live_agent` function, the transport layer routes the conference setup through the right provider-specific mechanism (Twilio conference, Plivo dial-up, Exotel flow) behind a single interface.
- **Daily.co for web workflows and playground mode.** The same pipeline, same templates, same observability — the only thing that changes is the transport processor at the edge of the pipeline.

This is the layer that, in practice, most merchants notice first. A voice product that cannot switch providers cleanly, cannot fail over during a provider outage, and cannot run the same template over phone and web with the same behaviour is a voice product that will have to be partially rewritten every few months. Ours does not.

## Would switching language remove the need for any of this?

A question we get occasionally: *if we rewrote the voice agent in Go or Rust, would we still need one-pod-per-call?* The answer is yes, with nuance.

A language switch would eliminate the GIL bottleneck, improve native multi-core utilisation, and — in Rust's case — remove garbage-collection pauses entirely. All of that would make Scenario 1 (many calls in one process) stronger in absolute terms. But none of it changes the structural constraints that motivate isolation in the first place:

- Kubernetes CPU limits are still enforced at the container level, and CFS throttling still freezes the entire container when the quota is exceeded.
- A single crash can still bring down every session sharing a pod.
- Memory pressure still affects every session inside the container.
- The noisy-neighbour problem still exists — in any language.

The container boundary is the real isolation boundary, and the container boundary does not care what language is running inside. Switching language is a **performance optimisation**; per-call isolation is a **correctness property**. For real-time voice in production, you need both eventually, but the isolation has to come first.

## What the runtime layer delivers

Put together, Agent's Cloud is the layer that turns a voice agent that works in a demo into a voice product that works in production. We are not at the theoretical limit on every axis — nobody is — but we are at the point where the dominant latency contributor in most calls is the LLM itself, not our plumbing. That is the right place to be: every improvement in frontier-model latency translates directly into improvement in the product without any engineering on our side.

---

# Evaluation and Iteration Loops

## What we already run in production

Every call produces a complete, structured trace:

- **OTEL root span per conversation**, named with a `customer_name-shop_name-timestamp` identifier and decorated with attributes covering the lead payload, the full conversation metadata, the final outcome, the call duration, and the attempt count.
- **Nested tool spans** via auto-tracing decorators, so every LLM call, function invocation, STT decision, and TTS request shows up as a child span.
- **Full transcription storage** on the lead record as an array of `{role, content}` pairs collected from the LLM message history at the end of the conversation.
- **Node traversal tracking** on the lead metadata: every node entry and exit, with IST timestamps, duration, the function that triggered the transition, and the arguments the LLM supplied.
- **Outcome storage** in both a canonical outcome column and a structured metadata dict, so analytics can slice by outcome and workflow.
- **Analytics endpoints** exposing per-merchant call counts, outcome breakdowns, duration distributions, transfer rates, and time-series trends.

On top of that, we run an **automated reactive evaluation loop**:

- Traces are exported via OTLP to Langfuse.
- LLM-as-a-judge evaluators score conversations on multiple axes (outcome correctness, latency, safety, escalation quality — the set is configurable via dynamic config).
- A background task — running on a 60-second scheduler loop, guarded by a Redis distributed lock so only one pod executes at a time — polls Langfuse every 10 minutes for new scores.
- Overlapping time windows guarantee 100% coverage even across pod restarts.
- Per-call deduplication (keyed by call SID) prevents duplicate alerts.
- When a score falls below a configured threshold, a Slack alert is sent with the call context, masked phone number, evaluator name, score, and a direct link to the call recording.

This is more evaluation infrastructure than most voice AI products actually run in production. It closes the loop between "a bad call happened" and "an engineer can see the full context and act within minutes". It is also, genuinely, a product advantage: when we onboard a new merchant, we can show them the alerting dashboard on the first day.

## The next step: proactive evaluation

The reactive loop tells us when something has gone wrong in production. The natural next step, which we have the raw data for and is near-term work, is a proactive loop that prevents regressions before they ship rather than flags them after — replaying historical calls against candidate template changes, structured A/B experimentation on production traffic, and pre-deployment regression gates on curated scenarios. A smaller related item already on the near-term list is context summarisation for long conversations, so that advisory and escalation calls do not grow the LLM context unbounded.

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Specific work items in this area

- **Replay harness.** Re-run a historical call against a new template or prompt and compare outcomes. We have the traces, transcriptions, and node histories for this; the harness that runs them through a candidate template and scores the deltas is the piece to build.
- **A/B testing infrastructure.** Split traffic between template variants, measure outcome deltas with enough statistical rigour to decide, and automatically promote winners. The template system already supports variants; the experiment framework around them is next.
- **Pre-deployment regression gates.** A curated set of scripted and real-trace-derived scenarios that every template change passes before shipping.
- **Uncertainty calibration on critical slots.** For address collection, payment confirmation, OTP readback, and account details, we capture STT confidence at the token level. Using it to decide "ask the customer to repeat" vs "proceed" becomes mandatory as we expand into regulated financial services.
- **Teacher trajectories.** Ideal conversation paths per workflow family, used as a reference signal to measure how far production calls drift from the intended path. The node-traversal data we already log is the right raw material.
:::

## Why this investment compounds

Every production call is private, India-specific, code-mixed, accented, noisy data. It cannot be bought and cannot be scraped. Closing the proactive loop is what turns this data into a compounding moat rather than passive storage: every merchant we serve makes every subsequent merchant's deployment more reliable, and over time the feedback loop is what turns today's engineering depth into a durable advantage.

---

# Where to Invest

The four-layer framing is also our investment framing. Each layer has a near-term improvement and a longer-term bet.

## Close the proactive evaluation loop

This is the highest-leverage incremental investment. The trace data is already there; the reactive alerting is already running. What is missing is the replay harness, the A/B framework, the regression gates, and the uncertainty-calibration work on critical slots. Each of those is a small, well-defined project. Together they turn "we know when something goes wrong" into "we systematically make the next deployment better than the last one", and they let us report outcome improvements to merchants with confidence.

## Specialised small models for bounded enterprise tasks

Frontier models are very good at open-ended reasoning and very expensive per token. A specialised small model — fine-tuned on our own traces for intent capture, slot filling, tool calling, and structured summarisation on a specific workflow family — can handle the bulk of the per-turn work at a fraction of the latency and cost, with a frontier model available as a fallback for anything the small model is not confident about.

The approach we are most interested in is layered specialisation: train and evaluate separately on language, conversation flow, tool usage, and policy/safety; iterate using failure-driven data from production; then merge into a single conversational SLM with regression gates across all axes. This is the kind of work that pays back slowly but permanently, and it is only feasible once the evaluation loop is in place.

## Orchestration depth: reusable flow primitives

The template system today is flexible enough that any merchant workflow can be expressed, but every new merchant still writes their own template from scratch. The next evolution is **reusable flow primitives** — address collection, payment confirmation, OTP readback, escalation handoff — that merchants compose instead of writing. This reduces deployment time further, concentrates improvements (a fix to the address primitive improves every merchant that uses it), and is the groundwork for simulation and automated testing of templates.

## A small, deliberate bet on the speech-native (duplex) frontier

The next model frontier is speech-in → speech-out without an intermediate text representation. Research on full-duplex speech-native models continues to progress. When this becomes production-ready for enterprise use, it changes the nature of turn-taking and interruption: conversation becomes continuous rather than punctuated, and a lot of the carefully tuned VAD / STT endpointing / interruption work moves inside the model.

We should not redesign the product around this today — it is not ready and the economics are not clear. We should **budget a small amount of engineering to prototype it** alongside the main stack, so that when it becomes viable we are ready to move quickly. The good news is that almost all of the engineering depth described in this document — the template layer, the orchestration, the state machine, the observability, the multi-provider runtime — carries forward regardless of the model substrate underneath. A speech-native model is a different pipeline processor, not a different product.

## India trace data as a compounding advantage

Finally, the data. Every production call produces a trace that is specific to India, specific to our merchants, and not available anywhere else. Without the evaluation loop, this is just storage. With it, it is a slow-but-compounding moat — the kind that takes 18 months to show up and then is very hard to dislodge. This is why closing the evaluation loop is high leverage: it is the unlock for everything else that depends on data.

---

# 8 Distribution as an accelarant and how the Advantage grows

This section is not about the voice stack. It is about what happens when the voice stack meets distribution — and why the combination matters more than either part alone.

---

## 8.1 Four layers, and why having all four matters

The previous sections describe four capability layers: orchestration and flow control, India-specific conversational quality, runtime tuned for live sessions, and evaluation and iteration loops. Each of these is, on its own, a time lead — not a structural moat. Any well-funded team with strong engineers can build any one of them in six to twelve months. The claim we are making is narrower and more specific: **having all four layers integrated, in production, serving real enterprise workflows across multiple verticals, is a compounding position that becomes structurally harder to replicate the longer it runs.**

That claim requires evidence. This section provides it — first by examining what the current competitive landscape actually has in place, then by describing the mechanism through which distribution converts a time lead into a durable advantage.

---

## 8.2 What the competitive landscape actually looks like

We have studied the publicly available information — product pages, technical documentation, GitHub repositories, funding announcements, customer case studies, pricing structures, and third-party comparisons — for the companies operating in the India voice AI space. Here is an honest, layer-by-layer assessment.

### Bolna AI

Bolna is the closest direct competitor and the one we take most seriously. YC-backed (F25), $6.3 million seed round led by General Catalyst in January 2026 ([source](https://www.bolna.ai/newsroom/bolna-bags-63-million-seed-funding-led-by-general-catalyst-to-build-indias-voice-ai-platform)), scaling from 1,500 calls per day at first commercial deployment (May 2025) to over 200,000 daily calls, with more than 1,050 paying customers across e-commerce, BFSI, logistics, recruitment, and education ([source](https://www.bolna.ai/newsroom/bolna-bags-63-million-seed-funding-led-by-general-catalyst-to-build-indias-voice-ai-platform)). GoKwik — a company that overlaps with our merchant base — is a published Bolna customer, processing 250K+ minutes and 400K+ customer engagements through Bolna's voice agents ([source](https://www.bolna.ai/customer-story/gokwik)).

**Layer 1 — Orchestration:** Bolna offers a no-code playground and API-based agent creation ([source](https://platform.bolna.ai/)). Their open-source repository — which they have acknowledged struggling to maintain alongside the hosted product ([source](https://github.com/bolna-ai/bolna)) — shows a JSON-based agent configuration system. However, the architecture is prompt-driven — agents are defined by a system prompt, provider selections, and tool integrations. There is no published evidence of a node-based state machine with LLM-decided but authoritative transitions, fire-and-forget side effects, or the kind of template-level flow control that makes conversations auditable and resumable at the node level. Their orchestration is agent configuration, not conversation flow control. For simple workflows (confirm an order, qualify a lead), this is sufficient. For multi-step, branching, enterprise workflows with compliance requirements, it is not.

**Layer 2 — India-specific conversational quality:** This is Bolna's strongest layer. They claim sub-500ms latency, support for 10+ Indian languages and 50+ accents, and intelligent model routing per call ([source](https://www.ycombinator.com/companies/bolna-ai)). Their YC page describes India-specific routing that selects the optimal model blend based on language, region, and use case — conceptually similar to our blended STT/TTS approach. They also use cost-efficiency techniques like pre-recorded inserts for common utterances and smaller models where possible. We should assume this layer is competitive.

**Layer 3 — Runtime:** Bolna's pricing page indicates a cap of 10 concurrent calls on all paid plans below enterprise, with calls beyond that limit queued ([source](https://blog.dograh.com/bolna-ai-pricing-breakdown-and-how-open-source-saves-70/)). Their open-source repository shows a Docker-based setup using asyncio ([source](https://github.com/bolna-ai/bolna)) — which, as we discussed in §5.1, creates deterministic latency problems under real concurrency. Whether the hosted product uses a different architecture is not publicly documented. The 200,000 daily calls figure suggests they have solved scale at the infrastructure level, though the concurrency model and isolation strategy are not described. We cannot confirm one-pod-per-call isolation, merchant-specific warm pools, or the kind of circuit-breakered runtime we run.

**Layer 4 — Evaluation:** Bolna's platform page mentions analytics and a dashboard. There is no published evidence of LLM-as-judge scoring, automated alerting, node traversal tracking, or a systematic evaluation loop. Their open-source repository does not include evaluation infrastructure.

**Summary:** Bolna has strong India-specific conversational quality (Layer 2) and is growing fast. Their orchestration (Layer 1) is prompt-level, not flow-level. Their runtime (Layer 3) and evaluation (Layer 4) are not publicly documented at the depth we have built. They are a serious competitor on speed-to-market and breadth of language support, and a less serious competitor on enterprise-grade conversation determinism.

### SquadStack

SquadStack is a different kind of company. Founded in 2021, 251–500 employees, approximately $25 million raised, headquartered in Noida ([source](https://www.entrepreneur.com/en-in/technology/squadstack-transforming-the-bpo-industry-with-ai/489098)). They position as a tech-enabled BPO — "AI + human" — rather than a pure voice AI platform. They handle 750K+ daily calls, serve BFSI clients (Bajaj Finserv, Kotak Securities, Axis Securities), and claim their voice AI passed a Turing Test for contact centres ([source](https://www.voiceaispace.com/tool/squadstack)).

**Layer 1 — Orchestration:** SquadStack's orchestration is tightly coupled to their managed service model. Enterprises do not build their own flows — SquadStack's team builds and operates them. This is effective for the managed-service business model but is not a self-serve template system. A third-party comparison notes that SquadStack's scalability is "fundamentally bottlenecked by their reliance on human hiring" ([source](https://www.ringg.ai/blogs/best-voice-ai-agents-for-indian-languages)) — the human-in-the-loop model that makes their quality high also limits their leverage.

**Layer 2 — India-specific quality:** Strong. They claim ≤0.8-second median latency, a 4.85 TTS Mean Opinion Score (96% of human voice quality), and support for language switching mid-conversation ([source](https://www.squadstack.ai/voicebot)). Their voice engine is trained on what they describe as "billions of structured conversations across India" ([source](https://itbrief.in/story/squadstack-ai-unveils-in-app-voice-ai-to-cut-drop-offs)). The BFSI client base suggests they handle regulated, high-stakes conversations competently.

**Layer 3 — Runtime:** Not publicly documented in technical terms. The scale (750K+ daily calls) implies serious infrastructure, but the architecture is not described. The managed-service model means they control the full stack — which is an advantage for their business model but does not produce a self-serve product.

**Layer 4 — Evaluation:** SquadStack recently launched "Optimize," described as a multivariate testing engine for voice AI campaigns ([source](https://www.squadstack.ai/)). This is closer to a systematic evaluation loop than anything else in the market, though it appears to be an A/B testing tool for campaign parameters rather than a full replay-and-regression system.

**Summary:** SquadStack is the most mature player in India by scale and client quality. But they are a managed-service company, not a platform company. Their moat is operational — relationships, trained teams, accumulated process knowledge — not architectural. They are not competing for the self-serve or developer-facing market. An enterprise choosing between us and SquadStack is choosing between "build my own flows on your platform" and "you run everything for me." Both are valid; they serve different buyers.

### BiteSpeed

BiteSpeed is a Shopify-native CRM platform (5,000+ brands, $5M ARR crossing to $10M) that added voice AI agents in late 2025 ([source](https://m.thewire.in/article/ptiprnews/bitespeed-launches-ai-voice-agents-for-e-commerce-sees-rapid-adoption-with-100-plus-enterprise-brands)). They are the closest competitor in our specific market segment — Shopify D2C merchants doing COD confirmation and abandoned cart recovery.

**Layer 1 — Orchestration:** BiteSpeed's voice is a feature inside a broader CRM, not a standalone voice platform. Their flows are pre-built for e-commerce use cases (cart recovery, COD confirmation, post-purchase engagement) ([source](https://m.thewire.in/article/ptiprnews/bitespeed-launches-ai-voice-agents-for-e-commerce-sees-rapid-adoption-with-100-plus-enterprise-brands)). They do not offer a general-purpose flow builder or a template system that supports arbitrary enterprise workflows.

**Layer 2 — India-specific quality:** They claim a 65% connect rate and "near-zero latency" ([source](https://m.thewire.in/article/ptiprnews/bitespeed-launches-ai-voice-agents-for-e-commerce-sees-rapid-adoption-with-100-plus-enterprise-brands)). The 65% connect rate is significantly higher than our current 55% — though this likely reflects different dialing strategies, number reputation management, or Truecaller verification rather than conversational quality. No published detail on multilingual support, code-mix handling, or provider blending.

**Layer 3 — Runtime:** Not publicly documented.

**Layer 4 — Evaluation:** Not publicly documented.

**Summary:** BiteSpeed is a distribution threat in the Shopify SMB segment — 5,000+ brands is a large base to cross-sell voice into. But their voice AI is a feature, not a product. It does not generalise to enterprise workflows outside e-commerce, and there is no evidence of the kind of engineering depth that would let them serve a Redbus or a Freshbus.

### GoKwik

GoKwik is an e-commerce enablement platform (15,000+ D2C brands) focused on checkout optimisation and RTO reduction ([source](https://www.gokwik.co/uk)). Their voice capability is interesting for what it reveals: **GoKwik uses Bolna as their voice AI provider.** The Bolna case study describes GoKwik processing 250K+ minutes through Bolna's agents for cart recovery, surveys, and collections ([source](https://www.bolna.ai/customer-story/gokwik)). GoKwik's own COD confirmation approach is primarily WhatsApp-based (OTP verification, captcha) rather than voice-based.

**Summary:** GoKwik validates the market (RTO reduction via voice confirmation is valuable) but is not building voice infrastructure. They are a customer of the voice AI ecosystem, not a competitor within it.

### ElevenLabs

ElevenLabs ($3.3B valuation) is the infrastructure-layer threat. They now offer a full Conversational AI platform with visual workflow builder, telephony integrations (including Exotel, Ozonetel, Plivo — all India-relevant), 70+ language support, tool use, and evaluation ([source](https://elevenlabs.io/conversational-ai)). They have an India-specific page highlighting Hinglish and regional speech, India data residency, and a forward-deployed engineering team ([source](https://elevenlabs.io/india)). Meesho — one of India's largest e-commerce marketplaces — is a published customer, using ElevenLabs TTS for automated Hindi and English customer support ([source](https://elevenlabs.io/blog/meesho)).

**Layer 1 — Orchestration:** ElevenLabs now ships a visual workflow builder that combines "scripted steps with dynamic agents" and lets users "customise behaviour at each stage" ([source](https://elevenlabs.io/agents)). This is moving toward the template-driven flow control we have built, though it appears to be a recent addition and the depth of the state-machine model is not clear from public documentation.

**Layer 2 — India-specific quality:** Strong and improving. 12 Indian language voices, Hinglish support, Indian-accent tuning ([source](https://elevenlabs.io/india)). They are a TTS provider first, which means their voice quality is best-in-class by definition. STT is not their core competency — they integrate with external providers.

**Layer 3 — Runtime:** ElevenLabs is a cloud platform. Their infrastructure documentation describes enterprise-grade scalability and high concurrency ([source](https://elevenlabs.io/enterprise)). The specific isolation model (per-call vs shared) is not publicly described.

**Layer 4 — Evaluation:** They ship agent simulations, A/B testing, and built-in analytics ([source](https://elevenlabs.io/agents)). This is more evaluation infrastructure than any India-focused competitor offers out of the box.

**Summary:** ElevenLabs is the most credible long-term threat. They have the capital, the model quality, and the platform ambition. It is worth noting that ElevenLabs is also a provider we use — their TTS is our default for English-first and English+Hindi workflows, as described in §4.3. This is not a contradiction; it is the nature of the layer we operate in. We are not competing with ElevenLabs on TTS quality — we are building the system around the model that turns a good voice into a reliable, auditable, enterprise-grade conversation. What ElevenLabs lacks is India-specific operational depth — the kind that comes from running thousands of production calls across Indian telephony networks, handling Exotel's webhook quirks, managing Truecaller verification, dealing with spam-flagged numbers, and debugging code-mixed conversations in noisy kirana-store environments. They are building from the model layer up; we are building from the operational layer down. The question is which direction closes the gap faster — and whether, as ElevenLabs' platform matures, the value of the operational layer above it increases (because more enterprises need someone to run it for them) or decreases (because the platform makes it easy enough to self-serve). We believe the former, especially for regulated and high-volume enterprise workflows.

---

## 8.3 The gap: no competitor has all four layers in production

The pattern across the competitive landscape is consistent. Every player is strong on one or two layers but appear to be  weak or absent on the others:

| | Orchestration (flow control) | India conversational quality | Runtime (isolation, failover) | Evaluation (systematic loops) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Bolna** | Prompt-level | ✅ Strong | Undocumented | Undocumented |
| **SquadStack** | Managed service | ✅ Strong | Undocumented | Partial (Optimize) |
| **BiteSpeed** | Pre-built flows | Undocumented | Undocumented | Undocumented |
| **ElevenLabs** | Visual builder (new) | ✅ Strong (TTS) | Cloud platform | ✅ Built-in |
| **Breeze Buddy** | ✅ Template state machine | ✅ Blended providers | ✅ One-pod-per-call | ✅ Reactive (proactive gap) |

This is not a claim that we are better than every competitor on every dimension. Bolna has more paying customers. SquadStack has deeper BFSI relationships. BiteSpeed has broader Shopify distribution. ElevenLabs has better TTS. The claim is that **no single competitor currently has all four layers integrated and running in production** — and that this integration is what enterprise buyers actually need when they move past POC into core operations.

---

## 8.4 Distribution is the accelerant

The four-layer advantage described above is a time lead. Left alone, it erodes. Bolna has $6.3M and YC's network. SquadStack has $25M and 500 employees. ElevenLabs has $3.3B and the best voice model in the world. We cannot out-fund any of them. We cannot out-hire SquadStack. We cannot out-research ElevenLabs.

What we have is **distribution that none of them can replicate in the near term**: Juspay's 100+ enterprise relationships across commerce, travel, mobility, financial services, and logistics — industries where voice is a core operational channel, not a feature. And Breeze Checkout's 2,000+ SMB merchant base on Shopify, where voice-based COD confirmation is a direct revenue lever.

The question is not whether we have distribution. It is whether we use it. Here is how each layer compounds when distribution feeds it.

### Orchestration → Template library

Every enterprise we onboard through Juspay brings a workflow we have not built before. Redbus needed operator verification. Freshbus needed post-journey feedback. Namma Yatri needs ride coordination. Each deployment forces us to build, test, and refine a template for that workflow family — and to extract reusable primitives (address collection, payment confirmation, escalation handoff, OTP readback) that make the next template faster to build.

After twenty enterprises across five verticals, we do not just have a template system — we have a template library. A new enterprise prospect in travel does not wait three weeks for a custom build. They get a composition of proven primitives, configured in a day. A competitor at template five is still writing each one from scratch. This is a deployment speed advantage that widens with every vertical we enter, and it is the kind of advantage that enterprise procurement teams can feel in the sales cycle even if they cannot articulate the architecture underneath.

The critical dependency is the reusable-primitives work described in §8.3. Without it, every template stays bespoke and template fifty is not meaningfully faster to build than template five.

### India-specific quality → Data-backed provider routing

Every production call generates a trace with STT confidence scores, transcription accuracy, TTS naturalness signals, and business outcome data. Across a hundred merchants in different cities, industries, and customer demographics, these traces become a lookup table: which provider works best for which language, region, noise profile, and workflow type.

Today this knowledge lives in engineering judgment. With distribution-driven volume and the evaluation loop, it becomes data-driven provider routing — and the routing gets more granular with every thousand calls. A competitor entering the market has to run thousands of calls across dozens of merchant types to learn what we already know. By the time they have learned what we know today, we have moved further, because our volume kept growing while they were catching up.

The critical dependency is the evaluation loop. Without systematic measurement of provider performance per workflow, the traces are storage, not signal.

### Runtime → Operational hardening

Runtime advantages compound differently — not through data, but through production scars. Every incident, every provider outage, every edge case we survive teaches Agent's Cloud something: circuit breaker thresholds get tuned from real failure patterns, warm pool sizing gets calibrated from actual merchant concurrency curves, zombie-pod cleanup catches failure modes we did not anticipate, and per-provider failover handles the specific quirks of Exotel's webhook format or Plivo's conference bridging.

After running a hundred thousand calls through Agent's Cloud, the system has survived things a competitor's freshly built runtime has not encountered yet. Enterprise buyers feel this as reliability — "their system just works" — even when they cannot articulate why. And reliability is the prerequisite for regulated industries (BFSI, insurance, healthcare) where a dropped call or a latency spike is not just a bad experience but a compliance event.

The critical dependency is volume. A runtime that handles 139 merchants, most on the free tier, does not generate enough production stress to harden meaningfully faster than a competitor at comparable scale. The Juspay enterprise pipeline — ten enterprises doing a thousand or more calls per day each — would generate more hardening signal in a month than a hundred SMBs generate in a year.

### Evaluation → Model intelligence

This is the layer with the highest long-term leverage and the most work still to be done.

Every production call produces a trace that is specific to India, specific to our merchants, and not available anywhere else — Indian voices, Indian accents, Indian code-mixing patterns, Indian conversational norms, across telephony-grade 8 kHz audio with real-world noise. Distribution gives us volume and variety of these traces that no other India-focused voice AI company can match, because no other company has Juspay's enterprise breadth.

With the evaluation loop closed, the sequence is: traces get scored by LLM-as-judge → replay harness runs candidate templates against historical calls → A/B framework measures outcome deltas → regression gates prevent regressions before deployment → specialised small models get fine-tuned on scored traces for specific task families (intent detection, slot filling, outcome prediction). Each rotation of this loop makes the next rotation faster, and each rotation produces a model or a template variant that is trained on data nobody else has.

This is the classic data flywheel, and it is the mechanism by which a time lead becomes a structural moat. After eighteen months of this loop running at enterprise scale, a competitor is not just behind on engineering — they are behind on data, and data is the one thing you cannot shortcut by hiring more engineers.

The critical dependency is closing the proactive evaluation loop described in §6.2. Every week it stays unbuilt is a week the flywheel is not turning.

---

## 8.5 What opens up beyond today's workflows

The product surface described in this document — outbound and inbound voice automation for commerce, travel, and mobility — is where we are today. It is not where the compounded stack ends up. As the four layers strengthen through distribution-driven volume, specific expansion paths become viable that are not accessible to a team with fewer layers in place or less production depth. This section describes those paths and explains which layers unlock each one.

### Regulated voice operations in BFSI

This is the expansion path where the engineering depth described in this document matters most — because regulated industries do not just want good voice quality. They want properties that most voice AI platforms are not built to provide.

India's BFSI voice landscape is being reshaped by regulation. TRAI's 1600-series mandate — which requires all service and transactional calls from RBI-, SEBI-, IRDAI-, and PFRDA-regulated entities to migrate to a dedicated numbering series — rolled out in phases from January through March 2026 ([source](https://www.mobicule.com/resources/blogs/trai-1600-series-new-mandatory-voice-rules-for-bfsi.html)).
